speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how you got to Magdeburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us, some kind of open train, or somehow. And we had women's barrack. And the men's was separate barrack. And this [INAUDIBLE] not only Jewish people was working-- French, and German, and Austrian. And we have the number, and the big cross in our coat. And the three of us-- it was a time, I cannot remember, where we don't give our maiden name. Somebody said if they not [? angered, ?] then they don't let together. So we three of us have three different name. And I work in machine. These all think I made it for the municipal, you know, for-- for the army or navy. And front of the other side was a little, round steel, and then put it in the machine. And my side-- here was the machine part-- I needed to put, and here came out this one, like a tube, and a very hot chemical water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they gave, every day, a new white gloves. We hardly get eat, but we get white gloves. Because needed-- it was so hot, needed to hold it. And this obermeister came, the first day, and told them I should be very careful. Don't cut, how I cutting, because front of me, a couple people cut their hand. And you know, my child, when you-- when they cut your hand, you-- doesn't worth it to feed you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we was there till April, because want to make it [INAUDIBLE]. And the nicest things was when they was with the bombing. Then, we went to the shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry-- sorry, I have to interrupt you. You mentioned that you went, also, to Stutthof. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before Magdeburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about that first?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was some place, I couldn't find it on the map. And when, two years ago, I was in Germany with Henry, I was think about Stuttgart. And I went to Stuttgart also, was a camp. But everything now is beautiful, green, and have a statue, memory of so many Jewish people. I really cannot describe more than-- middle of the night, was cold. But when the time they come to count us-- because every place they-- it was so hot. And some sand was there. And I don't know if same thing we did, like Torun, you know-- cut the grasses, or-- or work the kitchen. I can't-- I really cannot tell you. But we was in there too long, because they came-- the American people, that part. So they took us back to Auschwitz, I told you, when we get some new clothes, and took us to Magdeburg. And we work in Magdeburg till April. And was a big bomb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that happened in Magdeburg that you'd like to tell me, before?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] All the time, people-- especially German people know the area-- they disappeared. And we needed to [? knee. ?] And we were so numb, [? Cathy ?] every 10 people get killed. You never know-- you're going to be the 10 or you don't. Front of the barrack, and also it was winter. And my shoes, already, was gone. And I had-- had a wooden slippers or something. So my left leg and feet get ice, you know. So we were so sick, already, when-- what I planned to tell you-- when April was, and came back. And everything was burning, except the woman's barrack and the man's barrack. And somebody push me, you know, because always was a rush to go in our barrack. I think I fainted, or just fall down. But I was already so sick, and just a couple days before, they started who was with us-- cut with the knife, this. So I really don't care what happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next day, the men's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] broken up the-- where was the food-- I don't know what they call it in English, now. They have a-- a big storage place, when food was there. And they came and yell, you are free. You are free. We are free. So what you could do? Take your blanket, and what they bring in a carriage or anything. And we went to the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we saw all over, you know, where the bomb was. And in a round circle, I notice that my obermeister. Sold we ask him, in German, where is a bahnhof. Bahnhof, a railroad station. [INAUDIBLE] is no bahnhof for the-- my child, there is no bahnhof. Better if you go back to your barrack. At least you get something to eat. And next day, they came-- the Wehrmacht and SS, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. So they took us to the Elbe Bridge, because we in Magdeburg had-- and I don't know how many hundred women and men. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we was lucky, past the bridge. But they bombed the bridge. And who was behind us, they killed. And then the SS [INAUDIBLE] bark, and with the dog, with the-- lay down, lay down. By this time, we had some kind of vitamin or candy, and the 11 lager sister, we did a circle, or had this way. And we remembered our father said, from the first war, when a bomb hit one place, never hit same place. So people came, they bombed-- let's say this other corner. People runned, and they get killed. So by this time, we wasn't too many left. And again, was some SS and-- with us. And I don't know how many days we walk. And one day with, one day without. And we, in a dark-- almost evening-- somehow, the-- sometimes they let to stop us. You know, they said, rush, rush, rush. And was like a fork. And my sister, one of the lager-sisters, falled. And Erzsabet help her. But we don't realize, when they send us this way, then she's not behind us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we find that out in the evening, my younger sister and I, we really don't want to live, anymore. You know? You went through more than a year, and then lost her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember how many-- I think was two weeks, because May 8, when we arrived to there, there was two nice men with us-- one of them was in-- in the Red Cross-- and a couple of women. You know, one day we was with-- with some police, and next day was wasn't. One day we slept in a forest, next day in a barn. And so anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] May 8, we was walking and walking, in a rainy day. And we arrived to Zerbst. Every house was burned. But in the window was a white sheet, that mean they already liberated. And we walk, and if not these two men-- and a couple-- I don't think so my sister and I, we survive. Because they-- they did-- once in a while, you know, we was hungry. They put some bread, or something on the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the field. Then they know [INAUDIBLE], and then we should go and get it. And finally, we saw the American soldiers. And they took us to a-- a school, where many people was there, what we can eat. But that time, I was very sick. I couldn't hardly walk. And the next day, we was with friends, and Czechoslovak, and next day they said-- the-- who Hungarian, stand this way-- not like a appell. Who French, this way. Who English-- many Hungarian girls, who was smarter than us, went with the Swedish people. But we three of us was hoping-- our father was a strong, tall man. He survive, we want to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we get under the Russian. But one-- yeah, that was after. So anyhow, then they gave us some housing, a room. And they was very nice, some of the soldier. And what we did, close the door, and put a-- a table and a bucket of water, to somebody want to come in. You know, because many people this time, already raped, and already-- if not raped, just-- just want to get a piece more bread. And because was a doctor, already, who examined you. And they saw how I cannot. So a Jeep took us and my sister-- I don't want to go with her-- to a hospital, which is, actually, wasn't a big hospital-- for the German who came back from the front, you know, from the war. They have a relaxing place. And I get, every day, injection. And-- and was a Russian doctor, and she saw what-- you know, I get the yellow, long robe, and too big. So I cut it with a knife. And-- and asking-- so she ask me if I can [? sew ?] [? to ?] her dress, because to go into a big ball. So she took her room, and I make a black dress for her. And she said, why you don't want to stay here, and you go home? But before, I forgot to tell you, when we arrived to the hospital, one of our lager sisters was front of. And she said, my goodness. We was think about they killed you. Erzsabet-- six kilometer, or 20 kilometer, in another lager.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when this woman doctor want me stay-- we stay how long need to, because we want to go to this lager. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in this small hospital, we really get-- I get, every day, an injection, and-- and food too. And then we want to, when we was able to walk, to this lager. And again-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I want to skip certain things. I want to tell you that we get a house-- 11 us, you know, or maybe more. You know. And we used to go to the kitchen, where we get meal and help. And we talked Hungarian. And one day, some men heared us, to talk. They came from [INAUDIBLE], from the Joint. You know what this mean, the Joint, Jewish-- and we want to go home already, because they had some kind of story that the Russian people maybe take young people to Russia. And he said to us, after the eating he want to talk to us. And he came, I think, two or three men were-- he came and ask us where we live, and came and talked to us and ask who want to have the leader, and give some money. And was, I don't know where we were. This man came there. Wasn't even Jewish. And we said, the man is-- you know. So he said he has a railroad, and for this money you can get the ticket. And next morning, before light, we should go. Then nobody see us, to the rail. And this man wasn't there. But was a open wagon, some Yugoslav people just barely stop. And they let us, to climb to this place. And you know, one day we went this train, and stop. So we needed to wait. That was on the one bridge, you know, because it was in-- it wasn't too hot, you know. And all of a sudden, young Russian people-- you know, that was already end of the war. And because one of our lager sisters speaked fluently, German, so they don't want to believe that we are not German womans. We don't have the number. So one-- one Russian man, one officer, said-- because was talking, back and forth-- if you Jewish, then you know the Shema. That's the reason I would like all the Jewish children to learn the Shema, especially all grandchildren. When Jacob was three years old, I start to teach him Shema Israel, [SPEAKING HEBREW], just this much. And if we don't know that, I don't think so, we-- we get away. You know, a soldier is a soldier. He was very nice. He give some paper to us, and asking people, Joint-- Joint, or other city, them help us to get back Budapest. And how I said to you, one day we was waiting for come a train. So finally, we arrived to Prague. And they said have a Hungarian place, a Jewish, not Jewish. And middle of the night, I heard Hungarian anti-Semitism. And we went to the Joint, and they said, wait for two days. We give you some new clothes. Because in this little city, where we get in the house, from the blanket cover I did, for my two sister, a dress, and myself-- you know, should look. But we don't want to wait. We went to Bratislava. Also the Joint help us. And what was the custom, people who survive-- either for a tree or someplace where you could-- you put your name there. So I cannot remember where be put the [INAUDIBLE] girl's name. And my cousin, who was in Israel, who lived in Bratislava-- they went Budapest, and Bratislava-- back and forth-- because they heard we survived. Because before us, people much earlier arrived to Budapest. And in Budapest, the Joint have a place, [INAUDIBLE], to people like us who arrived there. They took our clothes, and it was [? lie on it ?] or-- or give something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this cousin of mine, where are we going to sleep? You know, so they took us a private place to sleep. And the first meal, they-- they-- and next day, I remember where my fiance mother lived, [INAUDIBLE]. And we went there, by foot-- you know, we don't have no money to take-- and we stayed there. She give her apartment. I think we stayed there about 10 days, still, when they fixed my teeth. When did you find out what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parent? They don't come back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We heard, after, that everybody who went right-- if you facing your left-- you know, Mengele facing them left, right-- then went, same day-- some stories tell-- same day at the crematorium. Some stories tell, the next day to the-- so that's the reason I keep shiva two days [INAUDIBLE]. My sister decide that the first day in New York, somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got back to Tokaj?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my younger sister boyfriend came back from the labor camp. And he came out to Budapest, to see us. We went back to Tokaj. And no place to sleep. And already, boys-- Morton stay with his cousin. I don't know if he tell. But one of my second cousin, and a couple boys-- Jewish boys-- occupy one of the Jewish house, when then-- the people, nobody came back. And we stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, we went to the city hall. And in the city hall, said here is [INAUDIBLE] bedding. Take what you want. And I ask, who was that? And the name was my former teacher. I said no, I don't want it. But they give me a [INAUDIBLE], [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And-- and one of the boy made it a nice sign. And how did the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the non-Jews greet you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them was very happy. Some of them, how you get back? How you came? You know, by this time, when we arrived to Tokaj, we wasn't so skinny. Because from May 8 till we arrive-- I don't remember, September 1, or August 30, or something-- then we gain, you know. We get so much injection, and then-- and in Budapest, already, my friend, you know, my fiance, they give me a scissors. And Morton stay with a cousin of her sister-in-law. She needed some dress.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Morton is your husband. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, next day when we arrive-- you know, like we arriving 4 o'clock in the morning with the train. And next day, they had a Jewish kitchen. And the people who came back, they went to eat there. I was a very proud girl, I honest with you. Next day, I already-- you believe it or not-- I cut a dress. And I get two student. One student parents, naturally. They don't want a two-years [INAUDIBLE] school. They want six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I don't taught her six months, because I get married. You know, yeah, it was six months. On girl bringed, every day, a little milk. That's what the tuition. And one girl parents, 2,000 kilogram wheat, and took to the mill. And so we had milk, and we had flour. And I don't want to get money for, if I designed a dress. I get a little oil, or some egg, or this. And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 12-- I told you I want to wait five years. And January 1, this girl-- what his name? What is name?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] . It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When I told you then, in Israel they have a-- a good friend of mine, [? Tsipora, ?] then New Year's Eve was. And my sister already get married, the younger sister, September. And Morton cousin, they was in there. He came out, and they knew he was came-- you know, middle of the night. And he took a plate. That is a Jewish custom, when you engaged they broke a-- because the five year over, then Morton and-- I remember, I cried, because my two sister here. And he-- I don't know why he get the idea then he should do it. So Morton, and I, and [? Tsipora, ?] we went to my sister house, and knocked the door, the window. Then we get engaged.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whose name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Morton Fuchs, but this time was [INAUDIBLE]. And I tell you some-- some happy story, because we talk many-- we talk many unhappy. My sister here in America, you know, I-- just [? cable, ?] was-- so somehow I sended that I'm going to get married. So they send it, a telegram-- don't get married, because easier to get out. Because they send some affidavit. Morton wasn't in Tokaj at that time. And the small town, in the post office-- they, don't get married, Hungarian Sunday, for [INAUDIBLE]. They was paid, the answer. So I sent that back, before Morton come home to Tokaj. Our wedding going to be February 12. My future husband named [INAUDIBLE], born 1911, June 30. Make the paper that way. Then my sister, the second [? with us ?] went school with Morton. So then they wrote to us, but if you get married, don't get children. But we got-- so [INAUDIBLE] we got-- because they remember the little [INAUDIBLE]. And-- and-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Fuchs-- I should say Dr. Henry-- Henry Fuchs, born 1948, January 20, named after Morton father, [INAUDIBLE]. And Marta, her-- she born in Budapest, 1950, June 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had. That's the reason we escaped. We escaped two reason. When-- after-- we always want to come to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oy, my goodness.
